data.description:
My thesis on American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) has transformed from a contrarian value play in August to a high-momentum, fairly valued opportunity. Q3 2025 results show significant brand and operational improvements, but I feel the current stock price potentially captures the majority of that success.

Q3 Earnings Review

The overarching theme of the Q3 earnings was a comprehensive brand and business reset that showed up in accelerated and profitable growth ahead of expectations. The beat on EPS and revenue is proof of strong execution in merchandising and marketing.

The growth numbers show continued improvements on the overall topline, a rebound set in motion in Q2, and now a healthy 6%. The comparable sales are also at 4% growth YoY now. The reversal from the flat/negative trends in the first half of the year is visibly validated in Q3. American Eagle comps have again returned to positive territory, up 1%, driven by the core denim business. Aerie comps are up by 11%, showing strong growth due to the focus on intimates. Brand health was strong, with American Eagle positioned at the top among jeans brands in the US (according to Circana Retail Tracking Services). Aerie is ranked 3rd in intimates, and the 'Offline' brand ranking is near the top for leggings.

The inventory levels rose by 11% YoY to $891m, compared to $804m in Q3 2024 (when it rose by only 5%). However, I see that as a positive, accounting for the higher guidance in Q4 (festive quarter). The inventory mix details should determine its positioning as weak or strong anyway, and the current trends should enable management to stock in accordance with the rebound and mix expectations. Also, the inventory is reported on a cost basis, which should be inflated because of tariff headwinds. The unit alignment could be more appropriate for the growth expectations in Q4 than the raw cost percentages suggest.

The below chart of operating margins is updated until Q2 2025 but shows the Q3 operating margin of ~8.3% is broadly in line with historical trends (barring the Q1 dip due to one-off impairment and restructuring charges). There is no breakout margin story over and above the topline growth happening at American Eagle.

To contextualize the growth in terms of how much room is left, the growth rates in Q2 2024 serve as good benchmarks because of the expanding topline scenario from 2023-24. Of course, there are greater revenue growth periods seen post-COVID and up to 2022, but that was due to pent-up demand across the board in specialty retail. Looking at the quarterly reports of the past couple of years, the comparable sales increase has been 5% at best in the past couple of years, which is close to today's levels. Revenue growth numbers have been around 8% in high acceleration periods. Healthy comps in expansion periods since 2022 have also usually been around 4-5% for Aerie and American Eagle. Overall, after the COVID-to-2022 boom, this has been the best quarter overall. There is some headroom for further growth (particularly the American Eagle brand), but in some cases, like Aerie, growth has been phenomenal by recent standards. The most significant forward-looking signal: Q4 operating income guidance has been raised to $155m-$160m (vs. previous $125m-$130m), with comps expected to grow 8%-9% due to a record-breaking holiday season start (management has already noted a record Thanksgiving weekend).

To balance the positives, there are a few drags to American Eagle's thesis that should also be discussed to understand how the valuations are factoring in net positives at the moment. A critical drag arises from tariffs, which continue to strain margins (eventually reflected in operating margins). However, despite the drag, gross margins have held up so far. While gross margins were up by 30 bps despite tariffs in Q2 2025, they were down by 40 bps YoY in Q3. Overall, margins will continue to be pressured even in Q4, where tariff impacts are estimated to be around $50 million, although impacts based on historical trends suggest a reasonable margin outlook despite the headwinds.

The celebrity campaigns, a very important factor in American Eagle's rebound, cost higher SG&A expenses. Q3 expenses were up by 10% YoY, and SG&A accounted for 28.4% of total revenues, primarily due to an advertising cost uptick. While the balance between topline growth and advertising spending will need to be monitored, it has worked out reasonably well so far with minimal damage seen in operating margins.

Digesting the Net Positives

The key marketing reach factor for American Eagle showed up in terms of the celebrity campaigns (Sydney Sweeney and Travis Kelce). They were massively successful, generating an estimated 40 billion impressions and driving a 700,000+ increase in new customers by Q2. The rebound is even more significant in a perceived slow demand environment for specialty retail. Along with the marketing efficacy, there are some structural merchandise fixes that are at play as well. Management confirmed that key categories were repaired - Aerie fleece is back, and intimates is accelerating. For AE, a successful pivot away from lagging categories (like shorts earlier in the year) into seasonally appropriate denim, sweaters, and fleece drove the back-to-school momentum.

While the improvements look healthy with some room for improvement (as per guidance), the headroom for further growth is limited looking at the historical data post the 2022-23 stagnation. I am not looking at expansion at the growth rates of 2020-22 because the economic scenario is not the same. Inflation is still not behind us, and rising costs of necessities have pressured consumer spending in the lower-income cohorts in particular (should affect part of the young adult customer base).

The overall spending pressures should make the outlook for discretionary purchases, like apparel, almost a zero-sum game. However, looking at overall specialty retail trends, Abercrombie & Fitch witnessed 7% sales growth in Q3. Gap sales also grew by 3%. While their improvements YTD are broadly in line with how American Eagle has rebounded, the latter's rebound is more meaningful given it was seeing negative growth rates not too far ago (even in Q2). This growth in larger specialty retailers overall is unlikely to be a sectoral tailwind given the macro situation. I attribute this to consolidation in specialty retail around bigger players and potential rotation between brands rather than a rebound in apparel overall. Apparel is resilient at best, but I cannot fathom a bullish environment going ahead. The overall outlook makes American Eagle's current and guided performance what should be assumed as "near-peak" levels. It could sustain the growth with improvements outlined for Q4, but it will not be the start of a post-COVID-like rebound.

Time to Book Profits

My original August stance on American Eagle was a contrarian value call - buy a depressed, under-earning retailer where expectations had collapsed. That thesis has now fully matured. The forward EV to EBITDA ratio has graduated to ~5x to ~11.2x now. The momentum may continue, but most of the turnaround has been captured. The valuations are now ~23% above the 5-year averages, which include both the periods of post-COVID rebound and the 2022 lull.

As the stock shows a post-market rally of ~10% after a great Q3 result (and outlook/guidance), I think it is a great time to book some profits. Hence, I downgrade American Eagle to a Hold. This is not a reflection on the quarterly results or the business' health. This approach is to sell on news after a bulk of the turnaround has been captured.

The Hold rating holds true for fresh investors too (who could not time entry at the bottom). I don't see the case for a significant brand-specific premium for American Eagle, looking at the broader sector-level resilience in most higher-end specialty retailers. American Eagle deserves credit for executing better, but its stock action is being pulled upward by a broader read-through that "US apparel is holding up" rather than anything structurally transformative. The improvements are still more heavily dependent on marketing intensity than on systemic margin expansion. The macro environment is far from healthy enough to support significant growth from here on. American Eagle's own historical trends show that its numbers are near peak levels now. All in all, it is prudent to book profits (sell into strength) and wait for further opportunities for re-entry.
